macro_segments.title: Introduction and Review of Previous Cases: Lav Hanitak La'aseh, Divorce, Non-Kosher Food, and Bal Teshaktzu

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and overview of Daf Yomi on Makkot 17; recap of lav hanitak la'aseh discussions
Discussion of cases involving multiple aveiros, eating insects, and the principle of bal teshaktzu (do not abominate yourself)

macro_segments.title: Eating Tevel and Separated Offerings: The Mishnah's Case and Rav's Position

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the case of eating tevel and grains from which terumot u'ma'asrot have not been taken
Rav's position: eating tevel that is only ma'aser ani (without other separations) incurs lashes
Explanation of tevel, ma'aser rishon, terumah, and the hierarchy of prohibited separations
The case of eating tevel when only ma'aser ani remains unseparated; connection to the concern of gezel (theft)
Tannaitic source (braitah) from Rabbi Yosei: derivation that ma'aser ani status still makes tevel prohibited
Scriptural derivation from Deuteronomy regarding ma'aser and eating in one's gates versus Jerusalem

macro_segments.title: Demai (Doubtful Grain): The Debate Between Rabbi Eliezer and the Sages

micro_segments.title: Introduction to demai grain from am ha'aretz (common people); explanation of what constitutes doubtful grain
The obligation to separate ma'aser from demai; monetary versus issur concerns
Practical separation process: ma'aser rishon, terumат ma'aser, ma'aser sheni, and ma'aser ani
Rabbi Eliezer's position: no need to designate ma'aser ani; Chachamim's response requiring designation
Gemara's analysis: determining whether the debate concerns tevel status or the practices of am ha'aretz
Conclusion: the debate is about whether am ha'aretz separate ma'aser ani, not about tevel status per se

macro_segments.title: The Quantity Required for Lashes in Eating Tevel: Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Shimon's Positions

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah's question: how much tevel must one eat to incur lashes; Rabbi Eliezer's position of no minimum
The concept of ke'briato (in its natural state) and why one grain might be significant
Gemara's debate: whole units versus quantities; ant versus grain as examples
Rabbi Shimon's position: no minimum amount for lashes; distinction between korban and malkus
Tosafot's explanation: the significance of a whole defined object versus undefind quantity

macro_segments.title: Cases in the Mishnah Incurring Lashes: Bikurim, Kodshim, and the Mother Bird

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah's list of cases incurring lashes: bikurim before kra (declaration), kodshei kodashim outside the Temple
Kodshim kalim (lighter sacrifices) eaten outside Jerusalem; the case of breaking a bone in a Pesach
The mother bird case (shiluach ha-ken): Rabbi Yehuda versus Chachamim on whether sending away negates lashes
The principle of ku'um asei bitul lav: when a positive commandment corrects a negative one, no lashes

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Comprehensive Derashah on Bikurim and Related Prohibitions

micro_segments.title: Gemara's statement that the Mishnah follows Rabbi Akiva's position on bikurim; why focus on Rabbi Shimon instead
Introduction to Rabbi Shimon's derashah on Deuteronomy 12, expanding the list of prohibited consumptions
The scriptural phrase 'lo tuchal le'echol bish'arecha' and its application to ma'aser sheni and other offerings
Terumas yadecha as bikurim: Rabbi Shimon's derivation that bikurim consumption before kra incurs lashes
Nedavot as todah and shelamim: deriving lashes for eating before zerikat ha-dam (blood sprinkling)
Bechoros (firstborn animals): lashes even after zerikat ha-dam for non-Kohanim
Bechors outside the Temple; kodshei kodashim (chatat and asham) with multiple conditions
Olah (burnt offering): prohibition on eating it entirely, even by Kohanim, after zerikat ha-dam

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Critique of Rabbi Shimon's Kal Vachomer Arguments

micro_segments.title: Rava's praise of Rabbi Shimon's brilliance in layering the derashah and establishing each prohibition
Challenge to Rabbi Shimon's kal vachomer arguments: ways in which earlier items are heavier than later ones
Examples: ma'aser vs. bikurim, todah v'shelamim vs. bechor, and the relative weights of different kodshim
Conclusion that kal vachomer arguments don't hold water, but Rabbi Shimon's ingenuity remains impressive

macro_segments.title: The Principle of Ain Mazirin Min HaDin and the Extra Verse: How Rabbi Shimon Derives His Conclusions

micro_segments.title: Fundamental problem: ain mazirin min ha-din—one cannot warn or prohibit based on kal vachomer alone
Rabbi Shimon's solution: the verse is yatira (extra); each word teaches a new lav for that item and heavier ones
Scriptural support: earlier passage in Deuteronomy lists the same items in positive (you shall eat them in Jerusalem)
Why repeat each word separately in the prohibition: to teach a distinct lav for each category
Conclusion: each word creates a separate prohibition (lav) that extends to weightier categories

topical_tags.term: bitlo v'lo bitlo / kiyum v'lo kiyum
Rabbi Eliezer vs. Chachamim (on ma'aser ani designation)
Rabbi Yehuda vs. Chachamim (on mother bird)
